tweets.text:
Rillet is making ERP AI-native:

- A ledger that's always accurate, complete, and reviewable is "continuously closed."
- That means no more month-end scramble. There's almost no manual work left at the end of the month.
- The manual journal entry is nearly extinct. Entries are increasingly posted without a single human keystroke.
- No business of a meaningful size will ever operate at 0% adjustments. Humans are still in the loop for small reclasses and accrual corrections.

Read the full breakdown: https://t.co/NUExKqdKEQ
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We built Rillet on the idea that the centuries-old practice of "closing your books" should be obsolete.

Month-end, which used to be a scramble to reconcile everything and get the numbers to zero out, is now just another day.

Based on a sample of Rillet customers, we went deep https://t.co/X1vjLdWvuM
tweets.full_text:
Rillet is making ERP AI-native:

- A ledger that's always accurate, complete, and reviewable is "continuously closed."
- That means no more month-end scramble. There's almost no manual work left at the end of the month.
- The manual journal entry is nearly extinct. Entries are increasingly posted without a single human keystroke.
- No business of a meaningful size will ever operate at 0% adjustments. Humans are still in the loop for small reclasses and accrual corrections.

Read the full breakdown: https://t.co/NUExKqdKEQ

tweets.text:
The gap between index volatility and individual stock volatility is rapidly widening:

The average S&P 500 single stock's 3-month implied volatility is up to ~40, the highest since the March-April 2025 market sell-off.

This metric surged +10 points since the start of 2026 and is also at the 2nd-highest level since the 2020 pandemic.

Volatility at these levels signals extreme stress across individual stocks trading within the market.

At the same time, the S&P 500's implied volatility remains near ~15, well below previous elevated periods.

This comes as mega-cap stocks dominate index weighting, suppressing overall volatility and masking underlying weakness.

The market is increasingly volatile under the surface.

tweets.text:
What just happened?

At 9:45 AM ET, the S&P 500 was trading nearly +1.5% higher and tech stocks were sharply higher.

At 10:40 AM ET, selling pressure began to intensify without any major headlines in one of the biggest reversals of the year.

Just 2 hours later, President Trump said Iran shot down a US helicopter "last night" and that the US must "respond to this attack."

This sent the S&P 500 to a new low of the day, down 240 points since 9:45 AM ET.

In just 3 hours, the S&P 500 has erased -$2.1 trillion in market cap.

Volatility is back.

tweets.text:
Rillet makes an ERP system that’s very different from competitors like NetSuite and Microsoft Dynamics: they “continually close” a ledger that’s always accurate, complete, and reviewable.

For companies using Rillet’s ERP system, the “Old Close” - meaning the bookkeeping entries that get manually reviewed and reconciled at the end of the period - is now just a tiny sliver of the ledger.

By and large, the books are being closed continuously, and that’s only recently become technologically possible.

Full breakdown: https://t.co/NUExKqdcPi

thread_tweets.text: @nicckopp And, sure enough, the type of entries you see on a ledger maps pretty reliably to what kind of data integrations are hooked up to the ERP: https://t.co/2oUhsugLJP
thread_tweets.full_text: @nicckopp And, sure enough, the type of entries you see on a ledger maps pretty reliably to what kind of data integrations are hooked up to the ERP: https://t.co/2oUhsugLJP

thread_tweets.text:
@nicckopp The “old-fashioned close” isn’t entirely gone, though; even with a modern setup like Rillet, there’s still some work going on at period-end.

Unsurprisingly, B2B companies have more than four times as much period-end work as B2C companies. https://t.co/U6gy1HuPFL
thread_tweets.full_text:
@nicckopp The “old-fashioned close” isn’t entirely gone, though; even with a modern setup like Rillet, there’s still some work going on at period-end.

Unsurprisingly, B2B companies have more than four times as much period-end work as B2C companies. https://t.co/U6gy1HuPFL

thread_tweets.text: @nicckopp So, just as B2B books have ~5x more period-end work, they also have ~5x more human-keyed entries, compared to consumer companies. https://t.co/dgitPmDIaZ
thread_tweets.full_text: @nicckopp So, just as B2B books have ~5x more period-end work, they also have ~5x more human-keyed entries, compared to consumer companies. https://t.co/dgitPmDIaZ

tweets.text:
BREAKING: Total onchain crypto card payment volumes hit a record $833 million in May 2026, up +180% year-over-year.

High performance networks such as Optimism and Solana have benefited from the growth in payment related activity.

These networks have outperformed the broader market since 2025 while capturing a combined ~22% of market share.

Key onchain players are accelerating adoption, such as Jupiter Spend through localized payment options such as QR integration, which has seen 60%+ month-over-month payment volume growth.

As a result, cumulative onchain crypto card payment volumes have exceeded $9 billion for the first time in history.

Onchain adoption is seeing rapid growth.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
The performance gap within tech stocks is at historic levels:

The top 20% of US tech stocks have outperformed the bottom 20% by ~120 percentage points over the last 3 months, the 2nd-highest reading in history.

By comparison, this difference peaked at ~135 percentage points during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble.

This spread has QUADRUPLED over the last year, outpacing the surge seen in 1999-2000.

Top 20% of tech stocks have now returned +110% over the last 3 months, nearly matching February 2000 levels.

At the same time, the bottom 20% performers have returned -10%.

The tech rally has almost never been this concentrated.

tweets.text: ‘There is a fundamental mismatch bt the growth of money in the economy and the amount of bitcoin available to buy. So what we look for when you want to be long crypto is when you expect an expansion of money and liquidity. The US debt is larger than the US economy and there’s historically one way govts get out of that situation: monetary inflation.’ - Jim Ferraioli of Schwab on what they look for as fundamantal case for bitcoin (which I totally i agree with, you HAVE to understand this to 'get' bitcoin imo, if not you'll see it al as pointless)

tweets.text:
Big day for @Morpho.

Last year, we sat down with @PaulFrambot and had him explain it from scratch: what it is, why institutions suddenly care, and where he thinks finance is headed.

Five answers 👇

thread_tweets.text:
Big day for @Morpho.

thread_tweets.full_text:
Big day for @Morpho.

Last year, we sat down with @PaulFrambot and had him explain it from scratch: what it is, why institutions suddenly care, and where he thinks finance is headed.

Five answers 👇

thread_tweets.text:
What is Morpho?

"Crypto is about exchanging money without banks. @Morpho is about lending and borrowing without banks." https://t.co/RmjH3zWOlx
thread_tweets.full_text:
What is Morpho?

"Crypto is about exchanging money without banks. @Morpho is about lending and borrowing without banks." https://t.co/RmjH3zWOlx

thread_tweets.text:
@Morpho Why are institutions paying attention now?

“Distribution is coming onchain. There’s a lot of money onchain that’s underserved in terms of yield, in terms of financial products.” https://t.co/oGI6fGvzt1
thread_tweets.full_text:
@Morpho Why are institutions paying attention now?

“Distribution is coming onchain. There’s a lot of money onchain that’s underserved in terms of yield, in terms of financial products.” https://t.co/oGI6fGvzt1

thread_tweets.text:
Is DeFi lending too risky?

“The way one should reason about risk when they get into those products is mostly two things. You have the operational risk, like literally, the code, is it safe? And you have the market risk, like, is the person that is borrowing the fund gonna repay?”
thread_tweets.full_text:
Is DeFi lending too risky?

“The way one should reason about risk when they get into those products is mostly two things. You have the operational risk, like literally, the code, is it safe? And you have the market risk, like, is the person that is borrowing the fund gonna repay?”

thread_tweets.text:
@Morpho Smallest hill the founder of @Morpho would die on?

(His answer is about croissants btw) https://t.co/80pD0FzKVW
thread_tweets.full_text:
@Morpho Smallest hill the founder of @Morpho would die on?

tweets.full_text:
Big day for @Morpho.

Last year, we sat down with @PaulFrambot and had him explain it from scratch: what it is, why institutions suddenly care, and where he thinks finance is headed.

Five answers 👇

What is Morpho?

"Crypto is about exchanging money without banks. @Morpho is about lending and borrowing without banks." https://t.co/RmjH3zWOlx

@Morpho Why are institutions paying attention now?

“Distribution is coming onchain. There’s a lot of money onchain that’s underserved in terms of yield, in terms of financial products.” https://t.co/oGI6fGvzt1

Is DeFi lending too risky?

“The way one should reason about risk when they get into those products is mostly two things. You have the operational risk, like literally, the code, is it safe? And you have the market risk, like, is the person that is borrowing the fund gonna repay?”

@Morpho How is onchain finance different?

“The entire inefficiency of the financial system comes from the fact that you have 50,000 databases that are asynchronously updating each other, that don’t trust each other.”

Onchain finance replaces that with a shared, open substrate. https://t.co/Djmcdje03J

@Morpho Smallest hill the founder of @Morpho would die on?

(His answer is about croissants btw) https://t.co/80pD0FzKVW

Unpopular view:

Japan has a currency problem that requires a stronger yen, not a bond problem.

The US has a bond problem that requires a weaker dollar and lower rates.

On June 4, average participation among S&P 500 healthcare stocks moved back above 30% after at least a month below it.

The prior 13 signals were 13 for 13 one year later. Median $XLV gain was 11.89%.
